questions.prompt: Which of these queries is most clearly YMYL?

choices.text: 'What is the recommended dosage of [common medication]'

questions.explanation: YMYL queries are ones where bad information could meaningfully harm someone's health, finances, safety, or welfare. Medication dosage clearly qualifies — wrong information could cause real harm. The others are low-stakes consumer decisions where bad info costs you a meal, a mouse, or a slightly worse fish dinner.

questions.prompt: Your general recipe site publishes one article titled 'Foods to avoid during pregnancy' and it suddenly drops out of search results after a core update. What's most likely wrong, and what's the right fix?

choices.text: Probably keyword stuffing — strip out some keywords and resubmit.
This is a YMYL page on a non-YMYL site. Likely missing: medical reviewer, credentials, primary citations, recent update date. Add all of those (or remove the article entirely).
Probably a robots.txt issue — check your crawl status.

questions.explanation: Pregnancy / health is squarely YMYL. The algorithm applies heightened E-E-A-T scrutiny to that query category — author credentials, primary citations, and review by qualified professionals all matter much more than they would for a standard recipe. A single YMYL page on a non-YMYL site needs YMYL-grade treatment to survive.
